title: QA: bash denylist is fundamentally bypassable substring matching (security theater)
description:
## Severity
Critical
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/tools/bash/policy.rs:82-136
## Violation
The denylist matches lowercased substrings against a hard-coded list. The module's own doc-comment admits this is "NOT a substitute for a real sandbox" (policy.rs:3-7). The pinning test `b5_gap_589_advanced_injection_not_blocked` (policy.rs:453-469) further confirms process substitution, IFS injection, and `/proc/environ` reads pass through.

Trivial bypasses for the canonical "rm -rf /" rule include: `r''m -rf /`, `\rm -rf /`, `eval $(echo cm0gLXJmIC8K | base64 -d)`, `rm$IFS-rf$IFS/`, or any newline-separated multi-statement.

Quoted (policy.rs:82-93):
```
/// Hard denylist of command patterns that are effectively always malicious
/// or catastrophic. Returns `Some(reason)` when the command is denied.
...
    const SUBSTRINGS: &[(&str, &str)] = &[
        ("rm -rf /", "rm -rf of root filesystem"),
```

Encoding a denylist this thin into a security boundary creates false confidence.
## Mandated refactor
1. Rename `validate_command` to `coarse_safety_screen` and document that it only catches naive prompt-injection.
2. Architect a real sandbox (firejail/bubblewrap on Linux, seatbelt on macOS, AppContainer on Windows) for production bash execution.
3. Require explicit opt-in for unsandboxed mode with loud warnings.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
